narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've put together a strong profile for law school, including a respectable undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is actually a bit above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 40, higher than the usual 36.70. Your early academic performance was also notable, landing you in the 9th decile for Year 1 grades compared to peers, though your Year 3 decile stayed similarly high at 9 versus the average of about 5.5. Unfortunately, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 59.6%, tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction hinges on a few key factors from your record, with your undergraduate GPA emerging as the single strongest influence pushing the model toward forecasting a bar failure. While your Year 3 school decile ranking worked strongly in your favor, countering that risk by signaling solid later performance relative to others, it wasn't quite enough to offset the GPA's drag. Your LSAT score added some additional weight toward the failure prediction, even though it's above average, because the model sees it as not fully compensating in this context. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile is competitive in areas like LSAT and deciles, but the model prioritizes undergraduate GPA as the top differentiator here, where yours, despite being solid, didn't stand out as exceptionally high among bar passers. As a full-time student like most applicants (over 92% in the dataset), that aspect aligned with the norm and didn't sway things much. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a supportive background—it's the model's data-driven read on bar success patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push it even higher or bolstering your GPA narrative with strong graduate-level coursework or explanations in a reconsideration appeal. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early, as students with your profile often succeed by addressing these exact gaps. With some strategic tweaks, a stronger application could flip this prediction next time.
